coredata.dc:title: Nano-level resolution determination of aromatic nucleus in coal
coredata.dc:creator: Huan, Xuan
Tang, Yuegang
Xu, Jingjie
Xu, Miaoxin
coredata.dc:description: Based on the oxidative cutting method of coal-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new idea to study the aromatic nuclei in coal is proposed: the dispersed aromatic nuclei can be obtained by eliminating the weak aliphatic and functional cross-links. The structural characteristics of coal aromatic nuclei were visualized based on transmission electron microscopy with nano-resolution. Distinct differences in structural characteristics exist among aromatic nuclei, which depends on rank. With increasing rank, the number of condensed rings along with the diameter of the singular aromatic nucleus and the aggregates increased significantly. In high-volatile bituminous coal, most of the aromatic carbon rings are curved and poorly orientated, and internal defects could be observed. The carbon rings in low-volatile bituminous coal are densely arranged and exhibit improvement in crystal orientation. Most heterocyclic compounds and amorphous carbon structures in the anthracite disappeared, and the arrangement and orientation of aromatic rings is the strongest, which closely resembles graphite structure. X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectra of raw coals showed the average crystallite diameter (La ) and the minimum aromatic nuclei sizes are in agreement to those observed under HRTEM, the aromatic nuclei had undergone a process of evolution from small to large and from disorder to order. The first direct observation and quantitative analysis of the geometric and the polycondensation characteristics of the aromatic nuclei structure in various rank coals will provide a new perspective for further research on the molecular structure of coal.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coal
Aromatic nucleus
Nano-resolution
Direct observation
Image analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of aromatic nuclei from coal
Structural order as determined by XRD analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41872175 ). Sincere thanks are due to the Analytical Instrumentation Center of Peking University for their experimental support. The views and opinions expressed in this paper are those of the authors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Background-subtracted X-ray diffractograms of demineralized coal samples; (b–d) curve fitting results for three Gaussian peaks.
(a) Raman spectra of demineralized samples; (b–d) curve fitting results for the first-order Raman spectra.
(a–c) TEM images of aromatic clusters in three coals showing a regular size and shape distribution. Scale bar, 50 nm. (d) The size distribution summary of aromatic clusters determined by TEM.
EDX analysis of the thin layer of black spots on a glass slide, prepared by drop casting, illustrates that carbon was the main element, with O and S also included.

HRTEM and 2D FFT patterns of a single aromatic cluster; the blue inset shows the line-profile analysis results. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

HRTEM images of a randomly selected aromatic cluster show the aggregation characteristics very clearly. The spatial chain arrangements of all carbon rings in an aromatic nucleus were manually extracted. Different colors denote different aromatic nuclei. The six aromatic cluster are all from the yellow squares in Fig. 3. FX-1 and FX-2 with different shapes, defects, and few aromatic rings obtained from the a-FX; XM-1 and XM-2 with significantly enhanced aromatization and rearrangement correspond to the b-XM; XFL-1 and XFL-2 were selected from c-XFL and have similar structure to graphite. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Typical HRTEM images of independent aromatic nuclei of XFL anthracite. Affected by shooting angle and resolution, six carbon atoms in a single aromatic ring show only three in (XFL-a) and (XFL-b), but all can be easily distinguished by symmetry. The recognizable aromatic rings were hand-painted and showed high orderliness.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
Note: M, moisture; A, ash yield; V, volatile matter; ad, air-dry basis; d, dry basis; daf, dry and ash-free basis; Ro,
max , mean maximum reflectance of vitrinite; O - oxygen; *, by difference.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD lattice parameters of three coal samples.
Raman data of three coal samples.
Statistical results of aromatic clusters size.

head.ce:title: Nano-level resolution determination of aromatic nucleus in coal

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Geoscience & Surveying Engineering, China University of Mining & Technology (Beijing), D 11, Xueyuan Road, Haidian District, Beijing 100083, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Geoscience & Surveying Engineering, China University of Mining & Technology (Beijing), D 11, Xueyuan Road, Haidian District, Beijing 100083, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Based on the oxidative cutting method of coal-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new idea to study the aromatic nuclei in coal is proposed: the dispersed aromatic nuclei can be obtained by eliminating the weak aliphatic and functional cross-links. The structural characteristics of coal aromatic nuclei were visualized based on transmission electron microscopy with nano-resolution. Distinct differences in structural characteristics exist among aromatic nuclei, which depends on rank. With increasing rank, the number of condensed rings along with the diameter of the singular aromatic nucleus and the aggregates increased significantly. In high-volatile bituminous coal, most of the aromatic carbon rings are curved and poorly orientated, and internal defects could be observed. The carbon rings in low-volatile bituminous coal are densely arranged and exhibit improvement in crystal orientation. Most heterocyclic compounds and amorphous carbon structures in the anthracite disappeared, and the arrangement and orientation of aromatic rings is the strongest, which closely resembles graphite structure. X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectra of raw coals showed the average crystallite diameter (La ) and the minimum aromatic nuclei sizes are in agreement to those observed under HRTEM, the aromatic nuclei had undergone a process of evolution from small to large and from disorder to order. The first direct observation and quantitative analysis of the geometric and the polycondensation characteristics of the aromatic nuclei structure in various rank coals will provide a new perspective for further research on the molecular structure of coal.

ce:section.ce:para: The organic structure of coal is composed of condensed aromatic and hydroaromatic nuclei connected via short alkyl bridges, ether linkages, and thioether linkages [1]. Aromatic nuclei are the main part of coal macromolecules. Structural statistical analyses and chemical and spectral analyses can provide useful information about the physical and chemical constituents, but they are indirect because the aromatic nuclei of coal cannot be observed directly.

The maturation and evolution of organic matter in coal are characterized by cyclization, dehydroxylation, dealkylation, cracking, cross-linking, and aromatization/condensation. It is now known that highly substituted aromatic rings are the main aromatic structures in high-volatile bituminous coal, whereas condensed aromatic nuclei are present in the low-volatile bituminous coal and anthracite [1]. At anthracite stage, the aromatic nuclei increase sharply and exhibit graphite-like characteristics [2]. To obtain direct evidence on the arrangement of aromatic sheets in coal, some scholars examined raw coal structure by extracting high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) micrograph images [3–8]. However, these “lattice fringes” are the “vertical slices” of aromatic layers stacked in coal. Moreover, the lattice fringes represent only the projection of the aromatic lattice stacking surface. Some scholars used ruthenium ion catalyzed oxidation (RICO) to degrade the organic matter in coal into soluble organic molecules and analyze their chemical composition [9]; they have also compared X-ray diffraction (XRD) with HRTEM to help understand the development and change of aromatic structures in coal [10]. These researches have been limited to indirect descriptions, given a lack of direct evidence for the appearance of aromatic nuclei in coals with different degrees of coalification. Due to the structural complexity and heterogeneity of coal [11,12], it is difficult to study their aromatic nuclei structure directly [13].

In the past ten years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been developed [14], which are nanoscale fragments of graphene [15]; their lowest diameter is consistent with an aromatic cluster (aromatic nuclei aggregate) in coal. Thus, there is the potential for using this approach to eliminate the weak aliphatic and functional cross-links in coal to obtain dispersed aromatic nuclei aggregates (coal-based GQDs, actually) [16,17]. These aromatic nuclei, which are stable and difficult to eliminate by a strong acid, can be directly observed with high-definition HRTEM. The aromatization process, aromatic structural defects, and edge characteristics of aromatic sheets can be described visually by comparing and analyzing the size, aggregation state, and orientation of aromatic nuclei in coal with different coalification degree.

The aromatic nuclei in coal is of principal interest in this paper. The microstructural parameters, such as lattice disorder, grain size, and aromaticity of three coals with different metamorphism, were obtained from XRD and Raman spectroscopy. The aromatic cluster derived from coals were obtained by sonicating a mixed solution of concentrated sulfuric acid and nitric acid, followed by heat treatment at 100 °C for 24 h [16]. With the nano-level resolution of HRTEM, we observed the aromatic nuclei in coal. The evolutionary characteristics of aromatic structures in coal are discussed with diffraction, spectral data, and HRTEM observations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of aromatic nuclei from coal
ce:section.ce:para: Materials: H2SO4 (95–98 wt%), HNO3 (65–68 wt%), sodium carbonate, polytetrafluoroethylene membranes (Anpel, 50 mm*0.45 um), and dialysis bags (membrane filtration products, molecular weight cut off = 3500) were purchased (Jingsheng Huiyi Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). All reagents were used as received unless otherwise noted. Ultrapure water was used in all of the experiments.

Aromatic nuclei were extracted from coal using the preparation method for coal-based GQDs [17]. Coal samples (approx. 0.3 mg) were ground to less than 200 mesh (75 μm) and put in a mixed solvent of 60 mL sulfuric acid and 40 mL nitric acid. The mixture was treated under ultrasonic conditions (30 °C) for 2 h and then placed in an oil bath at 100 °C for 24 h. After the oxidation treatment, the mixture was cooled to room temperature and 5 mol/L sodium carbonate solution was added to adjust the pH to 7, resulting in a clarified brownish red solution. The neutral mixture was then filtered through a 0.45-μm polytetrafluoroethylene membrane and the filtrate was dialyzed in 3500 Dalton dialysis bags against ultrapure water for 3 d. During dialysis, the water was changed every 12 h.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of as-prepared aromatic nuclei were taken using a JEM-2100F microscope equipped with a computerized imaging system. Microgram amounts of samples were diluted with ethanol under ultrasonic conditions. The samples were deposited on a copper microgrid, and the ethanol was evaporated to form ultrathin films transparent to the electron beam. HRTEM was operated at 200 kV, with a dot and line resolution of 0.19 and 0.14 nm, respectively. Firstly, an enrichment region of samples was found at a very low magnification. Then, a submicron size region was selected for observation at medium magnification, and the elemental composition was determined using energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analyzer. The elemental analysis was carried out in “point mode”, in which the beam is localized on a single area manually chosen within the field of view to confirm the proportion of carbon, oxygen, sulfur and other elements. Finally, single aromatic nuclei in these regions were observed at high magnification. To prevent irradiation damage caused by the electron beam, the current density was controlled to less than 5 pA/cm2. The lattice structure can be observed by increasing the aperture and sensitivity of the camera. Furthermore, a fast Fourier transform (2D-FFT) was performed from the HRTEM image.

The calculation formula for the coalification degree (p) is [27]:
p =
0.3975 -
d 002
0.3975 - 0.3354
× 100 %

Raman experiments were carried out on selected samples with a RS inVia laser confocal micro-Raman spectrometer manufactured by Renishaw Company, UK. An argon ion laser was used as an excitation source. The excitation wavelength was 532 nm and the laser output power was 20 mW. In order to avoid thermal damage caused by the laser beam, the power irradiated on the sample surface was controlled to around 1 mW. The scanning range of the spectra was 1000–3000 cm−1, and the spectral resolution was 2 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural order as determined by XRD analysis

ce:section.ce:para: Raman spectroscopy provides important information about the microcrystalline carbon structure, such as order degree and crystallinity. Fig. 2 a displays a comparative plot of the 532 nm excitation Raman spectra of the coal samples, normalizing by intensity size. The D peak at 1350 cm−1 is used to indicate the disorder and defect of the macromolecular structure, while the G peak at 1580 cm−1 mainly reflects the degree of graphitization; the second-order bands at 2000–2500 cm−1 are 2D and D + G [30]. In Fig. 2a, FX and XFL display the highest and lowest Raman emissions at the D peak, while the G peak shows the opposite. With the increase in structural order, the D and G peaks vary from 1350 to 1400 cm−1 and from 1580 to 1600 cm−1, respectively. The D and G peaks in coal are relatively wide, indicating that the size of the aromatic nuclei are small.

Due to the presence of shoulder peaks, in order to describe the structural characteristics of coal more accurately, detailed analyses were conducted on the first-order Raman bands by curve fitting (Fig. 2b–d); the main parameters of the Raman spectra were obtained, as shown in Table 3 . Among them, the G peak is the vibration of aromatic carbon atoms, and the D1 peak at ~1350 cm−1 corresponds to the C–C vibration of aromatic nuclear aggregates, which is related to the lattice imbalance at the edge of aromatic nuclei or in-plane defects caused by heteroatoms [30]. The broad band appeared at ~1200 cm−1 has been designated as the D2 band, which is produced by amorphous sp2 -bonded forms of carbon structures, and originates from small organic molecules, fragments, or functional groups in coal [31]. The D3 peak near 1500 cm−1 decreases with increasing metamorphism, whose attribution is still controversial [32]; it may be assigned to the C–C stretching vibration of aliphatic or alkene-like structures [33]. ID1/IG is used to measure the degree of graphitization. The higher the value, the higher the degree of graphitization.

Although the coal samples in this study contain microcrystalline structure, they also contain a large amount of amorphous material. Restricted by the structural complexity of coal, XRD and Raman can only provide the arrangement of “aromatic sheets” and reveal the average value of crystal structure parameters; it is difficult to give an accurate description of “a single aromatic nucleus”.

The EDX was used to identify that the black spots (Fig. 3a–c) were carbonaceous in structure. The samples were prepared by deposition followed by spin coating on a glass slide. The total number of atoms in the aromatic clusters was quantified by EDX analysis shown in Fig. 4 ; the atomic weight ratios of carbon: oxygen: sulfur in FX, XM, and XFL were equal to 71.27:21.47:7.1, 84.92:14.41:0.67, 90.7:8.01:1.29, respectively. The Na atoms on the surface of FX aromatic clusters were derived from uncleared sodium salts. The sulfur atoms were probably from H2SO4 oxidation that induces the doping of sulfur into the aromatic nuclei.

We found that the average La measured by XRD (Table 2) and the minimum aromatic cluster size observed under TEM (Table 4) are congruent. Assuming the La can accurately represent the size of a single aromatic nucleus in coal, the aromatic cluster lamellae observed under TEM should generally be aggregates of no more than five aromatic nuclei. XRD analysis showed an increase in the number of aromatic nuclei condensation rings and the decrease of alkyl side chains during coalification [28,34,35]. However, XRD can only provide the average parameters of aromatic nuclei and is restricted by the complex nature of coal, which makes it difficult to reflect their heterogeneity and condensation mode. At present, the pattern of aromatic nuclei in coal across the rank range has not yet been observed directly. The aromatic nuclei arrangement characteristics of the coal structure can be determined by observing and analyzing a single aromatic cluster by HRTEM.

A single aromatic cluster was selected randomly from the TEM images for HRTEM observation, as shown in Fig. 5 . The two-dimensional 2D-FFT pattern of the aromatic cluster is inset in the corresponding HRTEM image. In the 2D-FFT image of high-volatile bituminous coal FX, the hexagonal crystal structure is only faintly visible and symmetrical spots are difficult to determine. The well-formed crystalline structure and symmetrical spots can be seen in the low-volatile bituminous coal XM. Anthracite XFL has the most compact space arrangement of carbon rings, and the crystalline hexagonal patterns and symmetrical spots in the 2D-FFT image are the clearest. The results confirm that the aromatic nuclei in coal is of high crystallinity and increases with the degree of metamorphism. The blue insets illustrate the line-profile analysis of the aromatic clusters in which the distance between two peaks represents the in-plane lattice spacing of 0.238–0.2 nm; a lattice defect of 0.488 nm also appears in FX [36,37]. The lattice fringe spacing corresponds to the (1 0 0) in-plane lattice of graphene [38,39]. Note that the in-plane lattice spacing decreases slightly with the increase in coal rank, indicating that the sp2 clusters in the aromatic nuclei of high-rank coal became more ordered.

Fig. 6 shows the high magnification images of the area enclosed by the yellow squares (randomly selected) in Fig. 3a–c and their corresponding skeletonized images. The skeleton extraction is based on the HRTEM acquisition method proposed by Sharma et al. [3]. Adobe Photoshop, with the image processing toolkit plug-in, is used to analyze the HRTEM micrographs (Fig. 6, HRTEM images on the left and skeleton images on the right) and to extract the lattice fringes. Unlike previous work [3], we obtained the frontal appearance of aromatic nuclei in coals with different coalification degrees. It is difficult to distinguish the six-membered aromatic ring in HRTEM images because of the limitation in resolution, but the white light spots are recognizable carbon atoms and the black spots are the centers of carboatomic rings. The corresponding skeletonized images can show the aggregation characteristics of aromatic nuclei more clearly. The spatial chain arrangements of all carbon rings in an aromatic nucleus were manually extracted along the centers of the carboatomic rings in the skeletonized images using the same color. Different colors denote different aromatic nuclei. There are obvious differences in aromatic nuclei structure for different rank coals.

The coalification degree of FX coal is relatively low and the condensation degree of aromatic rings is the lowest among the three samples. Aromatic clusters FX-1 and FX-2 contain aromatic nuclei of varied quantities with a variety of shapes and few aromatic rings. Most of the hexagonal carbon lattices are curved, less compact and poorly orientated. It is speculated that there are a relatively large number of heterocyclic compounds or amorphous carbon structures on the edges of aromatic clusters, resulting in an imperfect orientation and planarity [40]. In the HRTEM images of FX-1 and FX-2, we observed two different types of defects. One is the Stone-Wales defect, that is, pentagon and heptagon defect (see FX-1) [7,37]. The other is Single Vacancies, formed by missing lattice atoms in a six-membered aromatic ring, leading to the existence of dangling bonds or isolated carbon atoms (see FX-2). The aromatization and rearrangement of XM-1 and XM-2 were significantly enhanced, and the preferential orientation of aromatic nuclei in the aggregate was more obvious, which tend to form a single aromatic nucleus with a longer chain length and more chain numbers. Since XFL belongs to anthracite, the HRTEM images of XFL-1 and XFL-2 show that the aromatization and rearrangement are further enhanced. Most heterocyclic compounds and amorphous carbon structures have disappeared. The number of aromatic nuclei in the aromatic cluster decreases significantly and the size of the single aromatic nucleus increases significantly. The alignment and orientation of aromatic rings are the strongest among the three coal samples, which closely resemble a graphite structure.

Before the 1990s, in attempts to describe the structure of coal, almost all authors assumed that the size of the ordered unit composed of no more than 2–4 rings [41]. When HRTEM was used to visually observe the structure of coal [3], a method of assigning aromatic sizes to the extracted HRTEM fringes was introduced to represent the diversity and distribution of aromatic units present in coal. This method assumes that the aromatic fringes are in the shape of parallelograms [42], and limited an 8 × 8 or 10 × 10 parallelogram to the maximum aromatic fringe [6,43]; however, this is not a simplified representation of aromatic structure in coal. The aromatic nuclei of FX and XM are small in size, low in aromaticity and poor in orientation. It is difficult to distinguish six-membered rings and their alignment characteristics in HRTEM images due to the influence of aliphatic chains or heteroatoms [12] and spherical aberration on TEM lattice resolution [3]. Fig. 7 shows the HREM of two independent aromatic nuclei of XFL anthracite, demonstrating that the carbon rings are characterized by linear type and that the six-membered rings with compact arrangements can be distinguished according to their symmetry. The number of six-membered aromatic rings identified in Fig. 7a and b exceeds 100, and the longest chain has 12 and 13 sequentially connected carbon rings. Coal is an electron-beam sensitive material. Exposed to TEM for a long time, it will become amorphous due to the damage caused by the high electron-beam's intensity or prolonged beam exposure [3]. The orderliness shown in Fig. 7 is obvious, indicating that the photograph was taken before it was destroyed, which also confirms the existence of graphite-like structures in anthracite.

Drawing on the preparation method of coal-based GQDs, the dispersed aromatic nuclei aggregates were obtained by eliminating the weak aliphatic and functional cross-links in coal with strong acid. The main conclusions are as follows:
1)
Using the nano-resolution of transmission electron microscopy, the structural features of coal aromatic nuclei can be observed visually and clearly, and the structural parameters can be measured quantitatively.
2)
The distinct differences in structural characteristics exist among aromatic nuclei, which depends on rank. In low rank coal, most of the aromatic carbon rings are curved and poorly orientated, and internal defects can be observed; the carbon rings in high rank coal are densely arranged and exhibit further improvement in crystal orientation, manifested by the increase in the number of condensed rings as well as the diameter of the singular aromatic nucleus and the aggregates. Direct evidence for the existence of graphite-like structure in anthracite was obtained.

Influenced by the structural complexity of the coal and resolution of transmission electron microscopy, more direct evidence of aromatic nuclei in coal needs to be obtained along with an improvement in the experimental scheme.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41872175). Sincere thanks are due to the Analytical Instrumentation Center of Peking University for their experimental support. The views and opinions expressed in this paper are those of the authors.
